title: Turkish Housing Market
text:
Turkey has experienced one of the world’s most dramatic housing price surges in recent years, driven by high inflation, sharp currency depreciation, post-earthquake demand shocks, and structural supply constraints. Between 2010 and 2024, the Residential Property Price Index (KFE) published by TCMB rose by nearly 4,800% in nominal terms. After adjusting for inflation, the real gain is far more modest and has recently turned negative.
This project investigates the Turkish housing market through four questions:

How have housing prices evolved nominally vs. in real (inflation-adjusted) terms since 2010?
Which regions have experienced the most extreme price growth?
How has housing affordability changed, measured by the m2 price-to-minimum wage ratio?
What is the relationship between USD/TRY exchange rate shocks and housing price movements?

Explore the full analysis on the Analysis page and dataset details on the Data page.

title: About
text:
About this site

1 + 1

[1] 2





 Back to top

title: Analysis
text:
NoteKey Takeaways




Nominal surge, real stagnation. KFE rose ~4,800% nominally (2010-2024); in real terms the index peaked at 153.7 in 2022 and fell to 118.4 by 2024.
Affordability collapsed. Months of minimum wage to buy 100 m2 rose from ~110 in 2010 to ~276 in 2022 – a 2.5x worsening.
Exchange rate pass-through is strong. Quarterly USD/TRY and KFE changes are strongly correlated; TRY depreciation episodes directly precede KFE spikes.
Istanbul and Aegean lead, Southeast lags. Five-year cumulative nominal gains (2020-2024) range from ~2,100% to ~1,400% across 26 NUTS-2 regions.
Supply is contracting. Building permits fell from 1.22 million units in 2015 to 549,000 in 2024 – a 55% drop.

title: Data
text:
We integrate four official Turkish data sources:









#
Source
Series
Coverage




1
TCMB Konut Fiyat Endeksi
Nominal and real KFE, NUTS-2 regional KFE
Monthly, 2010-2024


2
TCMB EVDS – Consumer Prices
CPI index for deflation
Monthly, 2010-2024


3
TCMB EVDS – Exchange Rates
USD/TRY monthly average
Monthly, 2010-2024


4
TUIK Insaat ve Konut Istatistikleri
Housing sales, building permits
Annual, 2010-2024



Direct links:

TCMB KFE: https://www.tcmb.gov.tr/wps/wcm/connect/tr/tcmb+tr/main+menu/istatistikler/reel+sektor+istatistikleri/konut+fiyat+endeksi/
TCMB EVDS (CPI, FX): https://evds2.tcmb.gov.tr
TUIK Housing Statistics: https://data.tuik.gov.tr/Kategori/GetKategori?p=insaat-ve-konut-116







Note



Why this dataset? Turkey’s housing market has undergone one of the world’s most extreme price cycles in 2020-2024, driven by a unique combination of currency collapse, triple-digit inflation, post-earthquake displacement, and supply-side contraction. This makes it an exceptionally rich case for understanding how macroeconomic shocks transmit into asset markets.

title: Turkish Housing Market
text:
Turkey has experienced one of the world’s most dramatic housing price surges in recent years, driven by high inflation, sharp currency depreciation, post-earthquake demand shocks, and structural supply constraints. Between 2010 and 2024, the Residential Property Price Index (KFE) published by TCMB rose by nearly 4,800% in nominal terms. After adjusting for inflation, the real gain is far more modest and has recently turned negative.
This project investigates the Turkish housing market through four questions:

How have housing prices evolved nominally vs. in real (inflation-adjusted) terms since 2010?
Which regions have experienced the most extreme price growth?

What is the relationship between USD/TRY exchange rate shocks and housing price movements?

Explore the full analysis on the Analysis page and dataset details on the Data page.

title: Turkish Housing Market
text:
2024 Snapshot


Show code
latest  <- annual_macro |> filter(year == 2024)
prev    <- annual_macro |> filter(year == 2023)
aff_now <- affordability |> filter(year == 2024)

tibble::tibble(
  Indicator = c(
    "Nominal KFE (2010 = 100)",
    "Real KFE (2010 = 100)",
    "Nominal KFE YoY change",
    "Real KFE YoY change",
    "Average m2 price (TRY)",
    "Average m2 price (USD)",
    "Months of min. wage for 100 m2"
  ),
  Value = c(
    comma(round(latest$kfe_nominal_2010base, 1)),
    comma(round(latest$kfe_real_2010base, 1)),
    paste0(round(latest$kfe_nominal_yoy, 1), "%"),
    paste0(round(latest$kfe_real_yoy, 1), "%"),
    paste0("TRY ", comma(aff_now$avg_m2_price_try)),
    paste0("USD ", comma(aff_now$avg_m2_price_usd)),
    round(aff_now$months_100m2, 0)
  )
) |>
  kable(align = "lr")

title: Turkish Housing Market
text:
Key Takeaways






NoteSummary of Findings




Nominal surge, real stagnation. KFE rose ~4,800% in nominal terms from 2010 to

After adjusting for CPI, the real index peaked in 2022 at 153.7 then fell back to 118.4 by 2024. Buyers largely paid an inflation premium, not a true wealth gain.

Affordability crisis. In 2010, a 100 m2 flat cost ~110 monthly minimum wages. By 2022 this rose to ~276 months – a 2.5x worsening driven by prices outpacing wages.
Exchange rate pass-through. The 2018 currency crisis and the 2021-22 depreciation both preceded the sharpest nominal KFE increases. Quarterly FX and KFE changes are strongly correlated.
Istanbul and Aegean lead, Southeast lags. Five-year cumulative nominal KFE gain (2020-2024) ranges from ~2,100% in the Aegean to ~1,400% in Southeast Anatolia.
Supply is in structural decline. Building permits fell from 1.22 million units in 2015 to 549,000 in 2024 – a 55% drop that signals undersupply ahead.

TCMB EVDS (CPI, FX): https://evds2.tcmb.gov.tr
TUIK Housing Statistics: https://data.tuik.gov.tr/Kategori/GetKategori?p=insaat-ve-konut-116







Note



Why this dataset? Turkey’s housing market has undergone one of the world’s most extreme price cycles in 2020-2024, driven by a unique combination of currency collapse, triple-digit inflation, post-earthquake displacement, and supply-side contraction. This makes it an exceptionally rich case for understanding how macroeconomic shocks transmit into asset markets.

title: Data
text:
Preprocessing Pipeline
Raw TCMB and TUIK data require aggregation and cleaning. The full pipeline is in R/01_preprocess.R. Key steps:
Step 1 – Manual extraction. Series were extracted from TCMB publications and TUIK statistical tables into data/raw_*.csv files, preserving original source values.
Step 2 – Tidying. All CSV files are read with UTF-8 encoding and reshaped to one-row-per-observation with consistent snake_case column names.
Step 3 – Derived variables.








Variable
Object
Formula




kfe_nominal_yoy
annual_macro
(kfe_nominal / lag - 1) x 100


kfe_real_yoy
annual_macro
(kfe_real / lag - 1) x 100


cum_2020_2024_pct
regional_nuts2
Compounded 5-year nominal change


months_100m2
affordability
already in source data





Show code
# R/01_preprocess.R -- run once before rendering
library(readr); library(dplyr)

rd <- function(f) read_csv(file.path("data", f),
                            show_col_types = FALSE,
                            locale = locale(encoding = "UTF-8"))

monthly_data <- rd("raw_monthly_kfe_cpi_usdtry.csv")

annual_macro <- rd("raw_annual_macro.csv") |>
  mutate(
    kfe_nominal_yoy = (kfe_nominal_2010base / lag(kfe_nominal_2010base) - 1) * 100,
    kfe_real_yoy    = (kfe_real_2010base    / lag(kfe_real_2010base)    - 1) * 100
  )

regional_nuts2 <- rd("raw_regional_nuts2.csv") |>
  mutate(
    cum_2020_2024_pct =
      ((1 + kfe_2020_yoy_pct/100) * (1 + kfe_2021_yoy_pct/100) *
       (1 + kfe_2022_yoy_pct/100) * (1 + kfe_2023_yoy_pct/100) *
       (1 + kfe_2024_yoy_pct/100) - 1) * 100
  )

city_monthly   <- rd("raw_city_kfe_monthly.csv")
affordability  <- rd("raw_affordability_annual.csv")
quarterly_fx   <- rd("raw_quarterly_kfe_fx.csv")
housing_sales  <- rd("raw_housing_sales_annual.csv")
permits        <- rd("raw_building_permits_annual.csv")
minwage        <- rd("raw_minimum_wage_history.csv")
kfe_new_exist  <- rd("raw_kfe_new_vs_existing.csv")

save(monthly_data, annual_macro, regional_nuts2, city_monthly,
     affordability, quarterly_fx, housing_sales, permits,
     minwage, kfe_new_exist,
     file = "data/housing_market_tr.RData")

title: Data
text:
The Final Dataset
Download housing_market_tr.RData
load("data/housing_market_tr.RData")  # loads 10 objects

Contents


Show code
tibble::tibble(
  Object = c("annual_macro", "monthly_data", "regional_nuts2", "city_monthly",
             "affordability", "quarterly_fx", "housing_sales", "permits",
             "minwage", "kfe_new_exist"),
  Description = c(
    "Annual KFE (nominal+real), CPI, USD/TRY, sales, permits 2010-2024 (17 cols)",
    "Monthly KFE, CPI, USD/TRY time series 2010-2024 (180 rows)",
    "26 NUTS-2 regions: annual KFE change % and 2024 median price",
    "Istanbul, Ankara, Izmir monthly KFE + m2 price comparison",
    "Annual affordability: m2 price, min. wage, months-to-buy 2010-2024",
    "Quarterly USD/TRY vs KFE for correlation analysis 2015-2024",
    "Annual housing sales by type (total, first-hand, mortgage, foreign)",
    "Annual building permits and occupancy certificates 2010-2024",
    "Minimum wage gross/net history with mid-year adjustments 2010-2024",
    "New (YKFE) vs existing (YOKFE) housing price index 2010-2024"
  ),
  Rows = c(
    nrow(annual_macro), nrow(monthly_data), nrow(regional_nuts2),
    nrow(city_monthly), nrow(affordability), nrow(quarterly_fx),
    nrow(housing_sales), nrow(permits), nrow(minwage), nrow(kfe_new_exist)
  )
) |> kable()

Preview – annual_macro


Show code
annual_macro |>
  select(year, kfe_nominal_2010base, kfe_real_2010base, cpi_yoy_pct,
         usdtry_annual_avg, kfe_nominal_yoy, kfe_real_yoy) |>
  mutate(
    kfe_nominal_2010base = round(kfe_nominal_2010base, 1),
    kfe_real_2010base    = round(kfe_real_2010base, 1),
    cpi_yoy_pct          = round(cpi_yoy_pct, 2),
    kfe_nominal_yoy      = round(kfe_nominal_yoy, 1),
    kfe_real_yoy         = round(kfe_real_yoy, 1)
  ) |>
  datatable(
    rownames = FALSE,
    options  = list(pageLength = 15, dom = "t"),
    caption  = "Annual KFE + macro series, 2010-2024"
  )








Preview – regional_nuts2 (26 NUTS-2 regions)


Show code
regional_nuts2 |>
  select(region_code, region_name, kfe_2022_yoy_pct, kfe_2023_yoy_pct,
         kfe_2024_yoy_pct, cum_2020_2024_pct, median_price_try_m2_2024) |>
  mutate(
    cum_2020_2024_pct        = round(cum_2020_2024_pct, 1),
    median_price_try_m2_2024 = comma(median_price_try_m2_2024)
  ) |>
  datatable(
    rownames = FALSE,
    filter   = "top",
    options  = list(pageLength = 13, dom = "ftp"),
    caption  = "Regional KFE change % and 2024 median unit price (26 NUTS-2 regions)"
  )








Preview – monthly_data (180 months)


Show code
monthly_data |>
  filter(year >= 2020) |>
  mutate(
    kfe_nominal_2010base = round(kfe_nominal_2010base, 1),
    cpi_yoy_pct          = round(cpi_yoy_pct, 2)
  ) |>
  datatable(
    rownames = FALSE,
    filter   = "top",
    options  = list(pageLength = 10, dom = "ftp"),
    caption  = "Monthly KFE + CPI + USD/TRY (showing 2020-2024; full dataset has 180 rows)"
  )

title: Data
text:
Data Quality Notes

KFE methodology revision (July 2024). TCMB revised the KFE from a quarterly to a monthly hedonic regression model, rebasing to 2023=100. The monthly series here preserves both bases (2023=100 and 2010=100) for comparability.
Regional data. NUTS-2 regional breakdowns (26 regions) are published as annual % change figures. Median unit prices are TCMB quarterly figures averaged to annual.
Sales data. First-hand / second-hand split is available from 2015. 2024 total reflects TUIK revised figure of 1,319,539.
CPI deflation. Real KFE uses TURKSTAT official CPI (2003=100 base, consistent series converted to 2010=100 for this analysis).
Missing values. Some regional 2024 figures are NA where TCMB had not published quarterly data by the time of data collection. These are coded as NA, not imputed.

title: Analysis
text:
1. Nominal vs. Real Price Evolution

1.1 The two stories of Turkish housing prices


Show code
annual_macro |>
  select(year,
         `Nominal KFE` = kfe_nominal_2010base,
         `Real KFE`    = kfe_real_2010base) |>
  pivot_longer(-year, names_to = "series", values_to = "value") |>
  ggplot(aes(year, value, colour = series, linetype = series)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(pal$accent, pal$secondary), name = NULL) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("solid", "dashed"), name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2010:2024) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title    = "Nominal vs. real KFE, 2010-2024 (2010 = 100)",
    subtitle = "Nominal growth is dramatic; real gains are modest and recently reversed",
    x = NULL, y = "Index (2010 = 100)",
    caption  = "Source: TCMB KFE; deflated using TURKSTAT CPI."
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))









The nominal index rose ~48x from 2010 to 2024. The real index peaked at 153.7 in 2022 – just 54% above its 2010 level – before declining. By 2024, real house prices were lower than in 2022, meaning buyers who purchased at the peak paid a substantial inflation premium with no real asset gain.


1.2 Annual change rates: KFE vs. CPI


Show code
annual_macro |>
  filter(!is.na(kfe_nominal_yoy)) |>
  select(year,
         `KFE nominal YoY %` = kfe_nominal_yoy,
         `CPI YoY %`         = cpi_yoy_pct,
         `KFE real YoY %`    = kfe_real_yoy) |>
  pivot_longer(-year, names_to = "series", values_to = "pct") |>
  ggplot(aes(year, pct, fill = series)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.7) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = pal$primary, linewidth = 0.4) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(pal$accent, pal$muted, pal$secondary),
    name   = NULL
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2011:2024) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_percent(scale = 1)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Annual price change: KFE vs. CPI, 2011-2024",
    subtitle = "Real KFE turned negative in 2018 and again from 2023",
    x = NULL, y = "Year-on-year change (%)",
    caption  = "Source: TCMB KFE, TURKSTAT CPI."
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))









The real KFE went negative in two episodes: 2018-2019 (currency crisis) and from 2023 onwards (inflation ran ahead of price growth). In 2022 the nominal KFE surged ~183% YoY, yet CPI at ~72% still limited real gains to ~111%.


1.3 New housing vs. existing housing


Show code
kfe_new_exist |>
  select(year,
         `All homes`      = kfe_all_2010base,
         `New homes`      = kfe_new_2010base,
         `Existing homes` = kfe_existing_2010base) |>
  pivot_longer(-year, names_to = "series", values_to = "value") |>
  ggplot(aes(year, value, colour = series)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(pal$muted, pal$secondary, pal$accent),
    name   = NULL
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2010:2024) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title    = "KFE by housing type, 2010-2024 (2010 = 100)",
    subtitle = "New homes (YKFE) consistently outpace existing homes (YOKFE)",
    x = NULL, y = "Index (2010 = 100)",
    caption  = "Source: TCMB KFE -- YKFE (new) and YOKFE (existing)."
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))











1.4 Cumulative growth: housing vs. inflation


Show code
annual_macro |>
  mutate(
    cpi_cumul = cpi_yoy_pct,
    kfe_cumul = kfe_nominal_2010base / 100
  ) |>
  select(year, `Nominal KFE` = kfe_cumul) |>
  pivot_longer(-year) |>
  ggplot(aes(year, value, colour = name)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(pal$accent), name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2010:2024) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(suffix = "x")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Cumulative KFE multiplier since 2010",
    subtitle = "Nominal KFE is 48x its 2010 value by 2024",
    x = NULL, y = "Cumulative multiplier (2010 = 1x)",
    caption  = "Source: TCMB KFE."
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))











1.5 Three major cities vs. national


Show code
city_monthly |>
  select(date, year,
         Istanbul = kfe_istanbul,
         Ankara   = kfe_ankara,
         Izmir    = kfe_izmir,
         Turkey   = kfe_turkey) |>
  pivot_longer(-c(date, year), names_to = "city", values_to = "kfe") |>
  ggplot(aes(date, kfe, colour = city)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(pal$accent, pal$good, pal$secondary, pal$muted),
    name   = NULL
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title    = "KFE by major city vs. national average (2010 = 100)",
    subtitle = "Istanbul and Izmir consistently outperform the national index",
    x = NULL, y = "Index (2010 = 100)",
    caption  = "Source: TCMB KFE (selected reference periods 2020-2024)."
  )

title: Analysis
text:
2. Regional Analysis (26 NUTS-2 Regions)

2.1 Five-year cumulative change (2020-2024)


Show code
regional_nuts2 |>
  filter(!is.na(cum_2020_2024_pct)) |>
  mutate(region_name = fct_reorder(region_name, cum_2020_2024_pct)) |>
  ggplot(aes(cum_2020_2024_pct, region_name)) +
  geom_col(fill = pal$secondary) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(round(cum_2020_2024_pct, 0), "%")),
    hjust = -0.1, size = 2.8
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = label_percent(scale = 1),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Cumulative nominal KFE change by NUTS-2 region, 2020-2024",
    subtitle = "26 regions -- Aegean coastal and Ankara region lead",
    x = "Cumulative % change (2020-2024)", y = NULL,
    caption  = "Source: TCMB KFE regional data."
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8))









The Aydin-Denizli-Mugla region (Aegean coast) recorded the highest five-year cumulative gain at ~2,180%, driven by coastal demand and urban migration from Istanbul. Mardin-Batman-Sirnak-Siirt lagged at ~1,380%, reflecting lower income levels. The range across 26 NUTS-2 regions is roughly 1.6x.


2.2 Annual KFE change heatmap (2022-2024)


Show code
regional_nuts2 |>
  filter(!is.na(kfe_2022_yoy_pct)) |>
  select(region_name,
         `2022` = kfe_2022_yoy_pct,
         `2023` = kfe_2023_yoy_pct,
         `2024` = kfe_2024_yoy_pct) |>
  pivot_longer(-region_name, names_to = "year_label", values_to = "pct") |>
  filter(!is.na(pct)) |>
  mutate(region_name = fct_reorder(region_name, pct, mean, .na_rm = TRUE)) |>
  ggplot(aes(year_label, region_name, fill = pct)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 0), "%")),
            size = 2.5, colour = "white") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low      = pal$secondary,
    mid      = pal$warn,
    high     = pal$accent,
    midpoint = 50,
    name     = "YoY (%)",
    na.value = "grey80"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Annual KFE change by region, 2022-2024 (%)",
    subtitle = "2022 saw nationwide triple-digit gains; cooling is uneven across regions",
    x = NULL, y = NULL,
    caption  = "Source: TCMB KFE. NA = not published by data collection date."
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8))











2.3 Median m2 price by region, 2024


Show code
regional_nuts2 |>
  filter(!is.na(median_price_try_m2_2024)) |>
  mutate(region_name = fct_reorder(region_name, median_price_try_m2_2024)) |>
  ggplot(aes(median_price_try_m2_2024, region_name)) +
  geom_col(fill = pal$accent) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0("TRY ", comma(median_price_try_m2_2024))),
    hjust = -0.08, size = 2.8
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = label_number(big.mark = ","),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.22))
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Median housing unit price (TRY / m2) by NUTS-2 region, 2024",
    subtitle = "Istanbul commands a 3x premium over Southeast Anatolia regions",
    x = "Median price (TRY / m2)", y = NULL,
    caption  = "Source: TCMB KFE quarterly unit price data."
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8))

title: Analysis
text:
3. Housing Affordability

3.1 Months of minimum wage to buy 100 m2


Show code
affordability |>
  ggplot(aes(year, months_100m2)) +
  geom_area(fill = pal$accent, alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_line(colour = pal$accent, linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(colour = pal$accent, size = 2.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 110.4, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = pal$muted, linewidth = 0.6) +
  annotate("text", x = 2010.5, y = 122,
           label = "2010 baseline: 110 months",
           colour = pal$muted, size = 3.5, hjust = 0) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2010:2024) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title    = "Months of minimum wage required to purchase 100 m2, 2010-2024",
    subtitle = "Affordability worsened 2.5x by 2022; slight recovery in 2024",
    x = NULL, y = "Monthly minimum wages (number of months)",
    caption  = "Source: TCMB KFE unit prices; TURKSTAT minimum wage."
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))









In 2010, a minimum-wage worker needed ~110 months (~9 years) of income to buy 100 m2. By 2022 this peaked at ~276 months (~23 years) – a 2.5x deterioration. The 2024 reading of ~307 months reflects that prices have continued rising faster than the minimum wage even after the wage was more than doubled in 2023.


3.2 USD-denominated price per m2


Show code
affordability |>
  ggplot(aes(year, avg_m2_price_usd)) +
  geom_line(colour = pal$secondary, linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(colour = pal$secondary, size = 2.5) +
  geom_area(fill = pal$secondary, alpha = 0.1) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2010:2024) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar()) +
  labs(
    title    = "Average housing price in USD / m2, 2010-2024",
    subtitle = "Dollar prices collapsed with TRY depreciation in 2018-2022, then partially recovered",
    x = NULL, y = "USD / m2",
    caption  = "Source: TCMB KFE; TCMB USD/TRY average exchange rate."
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))











3.3 Minimum wage vs. m2 price (log scale)


Show code
affordability |>
  select(year,
         `Minimum wage (TRY)` = min_wage_gross_try,
         `Avg. m2 price (TRY)` = avg_m2_price_try) |>
  pivot_longer(-year, names_to = "series", values_to = "value") |>
  ggplot(aes(year, value, colour = series)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(pal$accent, pal$secondary), name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2010:2024) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = label_number(big.mark = ",")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Minimum wage vs. average m2 price, 2010-2024 (log scale)",
    subtitle = "Housing prices grew faster than wages, especially post-2021",
    x = NULL, y = "TRY (log scale)",
    caption  = "Source: TCMB KFE unit prices; TURKSTAT minimum wage."
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))











3.4 Minimum wage history with mid-year adjustments


Show code
minwage |>
  ggplot(aes(year, min_wage_gross_try)) +
  geom_col(fill = pal$secondary, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_point(aes(y = ifelse(!is.na(mid_year_gross_try), mid_year_gross_try, NA)),
             colour = pal$accent, size = 3, na.rm = TRUE) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2010:2024) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title    = "Minimum wage (gross TRY) per year, 2010-2024",
    subtitle = "Bars = January rate; red dots = mid-year adjustment (when applicable)",
    x = NULL, y = "Gross monthly minimum wage (TRY)",
    caption  = "Source: Turkish Ministry of Labor and Social Security."
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

title: Analysis
text:
4. Exchange Rate and Housing Prices

4.1 USD/TRY vs. nominal KFE (quarterly)


Show code
base_usd <- quarterly_fx$usdtry_avg[1]
base_kfe <- quarterly_fx$kfe_nominal_2010base[1]

quarterly_fx |>
  mutate(
    usdtry_norm = usdtry_avg        / base_usd,
    kfe_norm    = kfe_nominal_2010base / base_kfe
  ) |>
  select(period, `USD/TRY` = usdtry_norm, `KFE nominal` = kfe_norm) |>
  pivot_longer(-period, names_to = "series", values_to = "value") |>
  ggplot(aes(period, value, colour = series, group = series)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 1.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(pal$secondary, pal$accent), name = NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(suffix = "x")) +
  labs(
    title    = "USD/TRY vs. KFE -- both normalised to Q1 2015 = 1x",
    subtitle = "Currency depreciation consistently leads or accompanies KFE spikes",
    x = NULL, y = "Index (Q1 2015 = 1x)",
    caption  = "Source: TCMB EVDS (USD/TRY); TCMB KFE."
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 60, hjust = 1, size = 7))











4.2 YoY change correlation: FX vs. KFE


Show code
quarterly_fx |>
  filter(!is.na(kfe_yoy_pct)) |>
  mutate(usdtry_yoy = (usdtry_avg / lag(usdtry_avg, 4) - 1) * 100) |>
  filter(!is.na(usdtry_yoy)) |>
  ggplot(aes(usdtry_yoy, kfe_yoy_pct)) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = factor(year)), size = 3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE,
              colour = pal$accent, fill = pal$accent,
              alpha = 0.1, linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(name = "Year", option = "D") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_percent(scale = 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_percent(scale = 1)) +
  labs(
    title    = "USD/TRY annual change vs. KFE annual change, quarterly 2016-2024",
    subtitle = "Strong positive correlation -- currency depreciation feeds into housing prices",
    x = "USD/TRY YoY change (%)", y = "KFE nominal YoY change (%)",
    caption  = "Source: TCMB EVDS; TCMB KFE. Each point = one quarter."
  )











4.3 Real vs. nominal KFE change over time


Show code
quarterly_fx |>
  filter(!is.na(kfe_yoy_pct)) |>
  select(period, year,
         `Nominal KFE YoY` = kfe_yoy_pct,
         `CPI YoY`         = cpi_yoy_pct,
         `Real KFE YoY`    = kfe_real_yoy_pct) |>
  pivot_longer(-c(period, year), names_to = "series", values_to = "pct") |>
  ggplot(aes(period, pct, colour = series, group = series)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = pal$muted, linewidth = 0.4) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(pal$muted, pal$accent, pal$secondary),
    name   = NULL
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_percent(scale = 1)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Nominal KFE, CPI, and real KFE change, quarterly 2016-2024",
    subtitle = "Real KFE has been negative in most quarters since late 2022",
    x = NULL, y = "YoY change (%)",
    caption  = "Source: TCMB KFE; TURKSTAT CPI."
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 60, hjust = 1, size = 7))

title: Analysis
section: 5. Supply Side: Sales and Building Permits
text:
5. Supply Side: Sales and Building Permits

5.1 Housing sales by type


Show code
housing_sales |>
  select(year,
         `Total sales`  = sales_total,
         `First-hand`   = sales_first_hand,
         `Second-hand`  = sales_second_hand) |>
  filter(year >= 2015) |>
  pivot_longer(-year, names_to = "type", values_to = "count") |>
  filter(!is.na(count)) |>
  ggplot(aes(year, count, colour = type)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(pal$accent, pal$secondary, pal$warn),
    name   = NULL
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2015:2024) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title    = "Housing sales by type, 2015-2024",
    subtitle = "Total sales peaked in 2020 (credit stimulus); mortgage share collapsed post-2022",
    x = NULL, y = "Number of units sold",
    caption  = "Source: TUIK Insaat ve Konut Istatistikleri."
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))









Sales peaked in 2020 at 1.5 million units, boosted by the government mortgage campaign. Mortgage sales collapsed from 601,466 in 2020 to just 158,486 in 2024 as interest rates rose steeply, with mortgage share falling from 40% to 12%.


5.2 Foreign buyer sales


Show code
housing_sales |>
  filter(!is.na(sales_foreign)) |>
  ggplot(aes(year, sales_foreign)) +
  geom_col(fill = pal$secondary) +
  geom_text(aes(label = comma(sales_foreign)), vjust = -0.4, size = 3) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2010:2024) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Foreign buyer housing sales, 2010-2024",
    subtitle = "Peaked at 67,490 in 2022 after citizenship-by-investment boom; declined sharply after",
    x = NULL, y = "Units sold to foreign buyers",
    caption  = "Source: TUIK."
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))











5.3 Building permits: the supply pipeline


Show code
permits |>
  select(year,
         `Permits (units)` = permits_residential_units,
         `Occupancy (units)` = occupancy_permits_units) |>
  pivot_longer(-year, names_to = "series", values_to = "value") |>
  ggplot(aes(year, value, colour = series)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 2.2) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(pal$secondary, pal$accent), name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2010:2024) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title    = "Residential building permits and occupancy certificates, 2010-2024",
    subtitle = "Permits halved since 2015 peak -- structural undersupply through 2026-2027",
    x = NULL, y = "Number of units",
    caption  = "Source: TUIK Insaat ve Konut Istatistikleri."
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))









Building permits fell from 1.22 million units in 2015 to 549,000 in 2024, a 55% decline. Given the 2-4 year lag between permit issuance and delivery, this signals structural supply shortfall through at least 2026-2027.


5.4 Interactive full sales and permits table


Show code
annual_macro |>
  select(year, housing_sales_total, housing_sales_mortgage,
         housing_sales_foreign, building_permits_residential_units) |>
  mutate(
    housing_sales_total                = comma(housing_sales_total),
    housing_sales_mortgage             = ifelse(is.na(housing_sales_mortgage), "--",
                                                comma(housing_sales_mortgage)),
    housing_sales_foreign              = comma(housing_sales_foreign),
    building_permits_residential_units = comma(building_permits_residential_units)
  ) |>
  datatable(
    rownames = FALSE,
    filter   = "top",
    options  = list(pageLength = 15, dom = "ftp"),
    caption  = "Annual housing sales and supply indicators, 2010-2024"
  )

title: Analysis
text:
Conclusions
Four structural findings emerge from this analysis:

The Turkish housing boom is largely nominal, not real. KFE rose ~48x nominally but the real index peaked in 2022 and has since declined. Buyers in 2021-2023 effectively paid an inflation premium.
Affordability has collapsed to crisis levels. The minimum-wage-months metric worsened 2.5x by 2022 and continues to deteriorate, reflecting a decade of supply under-investment plus income growth unable to keep pace with price inflation or currency depreciation.
Exchange rate risk is the dominant driver. USD/TRY depreciation episodes directly precede or coincide with the sharpest nominal KFE increases. Turkish housing has acted as a partial inflation hedge in lira terms, drawing domestic savings away from financial assets.
The supply pipeline signals prolonged undersupply. The 55% fall in building permits since 2015, combined with post-earthquake relocation demand and collapsed mortgage activity, means structural supply constraints will persist even if nominal price growth slows further.
